title: How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set
body:
Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.

Myfunction
|list length data |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data:= received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
        ifTrue:[z add: list]

How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?
stackoverflow_answer:
Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.

If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:

(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:

data do: [ :each | each < x ifTrue: [ z add: each ] ]

This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....
explanation:
In Pharo, you can check if an element already exists in a Set using the `includes:` method. This method returns a boolean indicating whether the element is present in the Set.

Here's an updated version of your code snippet:
```smalltalk
Myfunction
| list length data |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data := received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do: [:k |
    list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "Check if the element already exists in the Set"
    (z includes: list) ifFalse: [z add: list]
]
```
In this code, `(z includes: list) ifFalse: [z add: list]` checks if the `list` element is already present in the `z` Set. If it's not present (i.e., `includes:` returns `false`), then the element is added to the Set using `add:`.

Here's a breakdown of the `includes:` method:

* `includes:` takes an argument, which is the element to search for in the Set.
* It returns a boolean value indicating whether the element is present in the Set.
* If the element is found, `includes:` returns `true`.
* If the element is not found, `includes:` returns `false`.

By using `includes:` to check for the existence of the element before adding it to the Set, you ensure that duplicates are not added.

For example, if `data` contains the numbers `1`, `2`, `3`, `2`, `4`, and `5`, and you execute the code snippet, the resulting `z` Set would contain only the unique numbers `1`, `2`, `3`, `4`, and `5`.

title: How to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to parse a command and an int to make a "turtle" move on a board. I'm a bit overwhelmed, since it's not throwing an exception, and I can't even figure out how to open the debugger without one.

My code:

"only should begin parsing on new line"
endsWithNewLine:= aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        bo := ci at: 1.
        val := ci at: 2.
        "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        ^ bo = 'down' "attempted switch"
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [
              bo = 'left'
                  ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
                  ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'right'
                        ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                        ifFalse: [
                          bo = 'up'
                              ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                              ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ] ].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can do it the way you did it, and you can open a debugger by inserting a self halt statement into your code.

Usually, ifs, and moreover case-sytle ifs, are a bad sign. So what you can do is break the functionality into classes like DownMove, LeftMove and so on, and then each class will implement its own functionality when you call, for example, an execute: method that will do exactly what is needed by the command. But in your case, the approach would be cumbersome; moreover, you have very trivial actions.

You can use a dictionary with definitions. So imagine that you define an instance variable or class variable:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> [ :dist | turtle down: dist ] .
  'left' -> [ :dist | turtle left: dist ] .
  ... } asDictionary

Then in your code, you do: (moveActions at: bo) value: valInt. This snippet will give you a block (value) for a string (key), then you evaluate the block with your integer.

On the other hand, as the action pattern is the same and only message changes, you can map only the message names in the dictionary:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> #down: .
  'left' -> #left: .
  ... } asDictionary

Then you can ask your turtle to perform a message dynamically given by string: 

`turtle perform: (moveActions at: bo) with: valInt`

Also, if you want to rely on the similarity between commands that you read and messages that you send to the turtle, you can dynamically compose the message string:

`turtle perform: (bo, ':') asSymbol with: valInt`

Please note that this is not recommended in your case, as, first of all, you are coupling user input and your code, i.e. if you decide to change the user command from down to moveDown, you will have to change your method name from down: to moveDown:. Also, this approach allows the user to "inject" bad code into your system, as he can write a command like become 42, which will result in the code:

`turtle perform: #become: with: 42`

which will swap pointers between the turtle object and 42. Or you can think about even worse cases. But I hope that this meta excursion was good for you. :)
explanation:
I see you're struggling with implementing a switch statement in Pharo Smalltalk!

In Smalltalk, there is no built-in `switch` statement like in other languages. Instead, you can use a combination of messages and blocks to achieve similar behavior.

In your case, you can use a cascade of `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages, as you've already attempted. However, this can lead to deeply nested code, making it hard to read and maintain.

A better approach is to use a `Dictionary` to map command strings to their corresponding actions. This is often referred to as a "dispatch table" or "command table".

Here's an updated version of your code that demonstrates this approach:
```smalltalk
| commandTable |

commandTable := Dictionary new
    at: 'down' put: [ :val | turtle down: val ];
    at: 'left' put: [ :val | turtle left: val ];
    at: 'right' put: [ :val | turtle right: val ];
    at: 'up' put: [ :val | turtle up: val ];
    yourself.

endsWithNewLine := aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue: [
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do: [ :com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        bo := i at: 1.
        val := i at: 2.
        "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        "dispatch to the corresponding action"
        (commandTable at: bo ifAbsent: [ ^ self assert: false ])
            value: valInt ].
```
Here's what's changed:

1. We define a `commandTable` dictionary that maps command strings to their corresponding actions. Each action is a block that takes a single argument, `val`.
2. In the `do:` block, we split the command into `bo` (command string) and `val` (value string).
3. We use the `commandTable` to dispatch to the corresponding action based on the `bo` command string. If the command is not found in the table, we assert false (you can customize this behavior as needed).
4. We pass `valInt` as an argument to the dispatched action block.

This approach is more concise, easier to read, and allows you to add or remove commands and actions without modifying the underlying logic.

As for opening the debugger without an exception, you can use the `halt` message to pause execution and enter the debugger. For example, you can add `halt` before the `commandTable` dispatch:
```smalltalk
commands do: [ :com |
    ...
    "dispatch to the corresponding action"
    halt. " pause execution and enter debugger"
    (commandTable at: bo ifAbsent: [ ^ self assert: false ])
        value: valInt ].
```
This will allow you to inspect the values of `bo` and `valInt` before the dispatch occurs.

title: In Pharo/Smalltalk: How to read a file with a specific encoding?
body:
I am currently reading a file like this:

dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (dir / 'test.txt' ) readStream.
line := stream nextLine.

This works when the file is utf-8 encoded but I could not find out what to do when the file has another encoding.
stackoverflow_answer:
For Pharo 7 there's this guide for file streams, which proposes:

('test.txt' asFileReference)
    readStreamEncoded: 'cp-1250' do: [ :stream |
        stream upToEnd ].
explanation:
In Pharo/Smalltalk, when reading a file with a specific encoding, you need to specify the encoding explicitly. By default, Pharo uses the platform's default encoding, which is often UTF-8.

To read a file with a specific encoding, you can use the `readStream:` method with an additional argument, `encoding:`. This method returns a `ReadStream` that reads the file with the specified encoding.

Here's an example:
```smalltalk
dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (dir / 'test.txt' ) readStream: 'cp1252'. " or 'latin1', 'iso-8859-1', etc. "
line := stream nextLine.
```
In this example, we specify the encoding as `'cp1252'`, which is a Windows-specific encoding. You can replace this with the encoding of your choice, such as `'latin1'`, `'iso-8859-1'`, or any other encoding supported by Pharo.

Note that if you don't specify an encoding, Pharo will use the platform's default encoding, which may not match the encoding of the file. This can lead to incorrect character representation or errors when reading the file.

Some common encodings you might need to use include:

* `'utf-8'`: Unicode UTF-8 encoding (default in many systems)

title: Message Not Understood for existing method
body:
Ok, so it's like this:

receiver selectors

returns

#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)

But 

receiver expression

throws MessageNotUnderstood.

Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?
stackoverflow_answer:
You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.
explanation:
The issue you're facing is due to the way Smalltalk (or specifically, Pharo) handles message sends.

In Smalltalk, when you send a message to an object, the object's class is searched for a method that matches the message selector. If a method is found, it is executed. If not, the object's class hierarchy is traversed upwards until a method is found or the top-most class is reached, at which point a `MessageNotUnderstood` error is raised.

In your case, the issue is that you have a method with a selector `selectors` that takes no arguments, and it returns a value that includes a `#` character, which is used to denote a message selector. Specifically, the return value is `#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)`.

title: How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)
body:
I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31); one for the month (1 - 12); and a four-digit integer for the year.

( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )

Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?
stackoverflow_answer:
If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression

Date year: 2015 month: 12 day: 31

and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).
explanation:
In Pharo and Squeak, you can create a `Date` or `DateAndTime` object by using the `year:month:day:` or `year:month:day:hour:minute:second:` method respectively.

Here's an example:

**For `Date` object:**
```smalltalk
date := Date year: 2022 month: 6 day: 15.
```
This will create a `Date` object with the values: year = 2022, month = 6, and day = 15.

**For `DateAndTime` object:**
```smalltalk
dateAndTime := DateAndTime year: 2022 month: 6 day: 15 hour: 12 minute: 30 second: 0.
```
This will create a `DateAndTime` object with the values: year = 2022, month = 6, day = 15, hour = 12, minute = 30, and second = 0.

Note that these methods are part of the `Date` and `DateAndTime` class, respectively, and can be used to create new instances with the specified values.

You can also use the `readFrom:` method to create a `Date` or `DateAndTime` object from a string representation, like this:
```smalltalk
date := Date readFrom: '2022-06-15'.
dateAndTime := DateAndTime readFrom: '2022-06-15 12:30:00'.
```
However, the `year:month:day:` and `year:month:day:hour:minute:second:` methods are more concise and efficient ways to create `Date` and `DateAndTime` objects with specific values.

title: How to sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk?
body:
I've got a Dictionary like this:

a PluggableDictionary(
    Rankable1->8.5
    Rankable2->9.0
)

I need just an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order:

a OrderedCollection(
    Rankable2
    Rankable1
)

I noticed it is easy to sort by keys, but I found it a bit more difficult to sort by values. What is the smalltalk way of doing this?
stackoverflow_answer:
If you need one shot sorted collection in noncritical loop you might use something like this (uses pharo syntax to initialize example dictionary):

pd := PluggableDictionary newFromPairs: { 'a' . 2 . 'b' . 1 . 'c' . 3} . 

(pd associations asSortedCollection: [:x :y | x value < y value]) 
            collect: [:assoc | assoc key].

If you would need it more often, than you might consider introducing your own class that will keep this collection calculated.
explanation:
A Smalltalk question!

In Smalltalk, you can sort a Dictionary by values using the ` associationsSortedBy:` method, which returns an `OrderedCollection` of `Association` objects, where each `Association` represents a key-value pair in the Dictionary.

Here's the Smalltalk way to do it:
```smalltalk
| dict sortedCollection |

dict := PluggableDictionary new
    at: 'Rankable1' put: 8.5;
    at: 'Rankable2' put: 9.0;
    yourself.

sortedCollection := dict associationsSortedBy: [:assoc | assoc value] descending.

sortedCollection collect: [:assoc | assoc key]
```
Let's break it down:

1. `dict associationsSortedBy: [:assoc | assoc value]`: This sends the `associationsSortedBy:` message to the Dictionary, with a block that takes an `Association` object as an argument. The block returns the value of the `Association` (i.e., the value in the Dictionary). The `associationsSortedBy:` method returns an `OrderedCollection` of `Association` objects sorted by the values.
2. `descending`: This is a keyword argument that specifies the sorting order. In this case, we want to sort in descending order (highest value first).
3. `sortedCollection collect: [:assoc | assoc key]`: This takes the sorted `OrderedCollection` of `Association` objects and collects the keys (Rankable objects) into a new `OrderedCollection`.

The resulting `sortedCollection` will contain the Rankable objects in descending order of their values:
```smalltalk
a OrderedCollection(
    Rankable2
    Rankable1
)
```

body:

Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.

If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

array1 with: array2 do:
    [ :a :b | a == b ifFalse: [ ^ false ]].

title: Squeak or Pharo for the beginning Smalltalker?
body:
I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.

Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?
stackoverflow_answer:
To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.

Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.
explanation:
As a Rubyist starting to explore Smalltalk, you're considering Squeak or Pharo as your implementation of choice. Both are popular and well-established options, but they have some key differences.

Pharo is a modern, open-source, dynamically typed language inspired by Smalltalk (p. 1). It was forked from Squeak in 2008 and has since evolved into a highly portable and flexible platform, running on various operating systems, including OS X, Windows, Linux, Android, iOS, and Raspberry Pi (p. 2). Pharo's virtual machine is written entirely in a subset of Pharo itself, making it easy to simulate, debug, analyze, and change from within Pharo itself (p. 2).

One of Pharo's core principles is to reinvent the essence of Smalltalk through evolutionary and incremental changes, rather than attempting a big-bang approach (p. 2). This has resulted in a robust and stable platform for research and development into dynamic languages and environments. Pharo serves as the reference implementation for the Seaside web development framework (p. 1 and p. 2).

In contrast, Squeak is a modern implementation of Smalltalk-80, from which Pharo was forked (p. 2). While Squeak is still an active and popular platform, Pharo has gained significant traction in recent years, with a growing community and a wide range of innovative projects, including multimedia applications, educational platforms, and commercial web development environments (p. 2).

When deciding between Squeak and Pharo, consider the following pros and cons:

**Pharo:**

Pros:

* Highly portable and flexible platform
* Written entirely in Pharo, making it easy to simulate, debug, analyze, and change from within Pharo itself
* Growing community and a wide range of innovative projects
* Robust and stable platform for research and development into dynamic languages and environments

Cons:

* May have a steeper learning curve due to its unique approach to Smalltalk

**Squeak:**

Pros:

* More established and mature platform
* Wider range of existing resources and documentation
* Still an active and popular platform

Cons:

* May be less portable and flexible than Pharo
* Less innovative and less actively developed than Pharo

Ultimately, the choice between Squeak and Pharo depends on your personal preferences and goals. If you're looking for a more modern, flexible, and innovative platform with a growing community, Pharo might be the better choice. However, if you prefer a more established and mature platform with a wider range of existing resources, Squeak could be the way to go.

References:

* Pharo By Example, pages 1-2, 7
* Pharo By Example, Preface, pages 1-2

title: Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?
body:
Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?

Essentially I'd like something along the lines of:

renderThisOn: aString

aString append: 'whatever text I want'

Essentially I'd like a String instance (ByteString, etc) to behave like the "html" object in Seaside. I pass it on as an argument to multiple methods, each adding some information to it.
stackoverflow_answer:
From a practical viewpoint the answer would be no, it is not possible to change the size of a String. You can modify the characters of the String though:

a := 'abc'.
a at: 2 put: $x.
a = 'axc' "true"

Therefore, when you concatenate two strings you get a third one, while the others two remain unchanged

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
u := s , t.
s = 'abc'. "true"
t = 'def'. "true"

Having said this, there is actually a way to do grow (or shrink) a String. The idea is to use become: (or becomeForward:). This message will replace all references to the receiver with references to the argument. In your case:

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
assoc := s -> 3 "referene to s"
s become: s , t.
s = 'abcdef'. "true"
assoc key == s "true"

The reason why I started my answer by saying that you cannot change the string's size is because in the vast majority of cases the use of become: is overkilling and the recommended practice is to review the code and eliminate the need for modifying the structure of an object.
explanation:
According to the textbook "Pharo By Example", it is not possible to add a string to a String instance via a method in the same way that you can with a WriteStream or a collection. 

However, you can modify an existing string using various methods such as `at:put:`, `replaceAll:with:`, or `replaceFrom:to:with:` (p. 210). For example, you can use `at:put:` to change a character at a specific position in the string:

`s at: 4 put: $h; at: 5 put: $u.`

This will modify the string `s` to `'no hurries'`.

Another way to concatenate strings is by using the comma method, which is defined by Collection, so it will work for any kind of collection (p. 210):

`s := 'no', ' ', 'worries'.`

This will concatenate the strings and result in `'no worries'`.

Using a WriteStream is often the best way to concatenate characters (p. 228). You can create a WriteStream and use `nextPut:` or `nextPutAll:` to add characters to the stream, and then convert the stream to a string. For example:

`String streamContents: [ :tempStream | (1 to: 100000) do: [:i | tempStream nextPutAll: i asString; space ] ].`

This will create a string with the numbers from 1 to 100000, separated by spaces.

In summary, while you can't directly add a string to a String instance via a method, you can modify an existing string using various methods, or use a WriteStream to concatenate characters and then convert the stream to a string.

title: Check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it
body:
Is there an easy way to do this in Smalltalk? I'm 80% sure that there is some method but can't find it anywhere.

I know that I can use

(instance class = SomeClass) ifTrue:

And I know that I can use superclass etc... but I hope that there is something built in :)
stackoverflow_answer:
To test whether anObject is instance of aClass:

(anObject isMemberOf: aClass)

To test whether it is an instance of aClass or one of it subclasses:

(anObject isKindOf: aClass)
explanation:
According to the textbook "Pharo By Example", you can use the `isKindOf:` method to check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it. This method is defined in the `Object` class and returns `true` if the receiver's class is either the same as, or a subclass of, the argument class (p. 183-184).

For example:
```
1 isKindOf: SmallInteger
>>> true
1 isKindOf: Integer
>>> true
1 isKindOf: Number
>>> true
1 isKindOf: Object
>>> true
1 isKindOf: String
>>> false
```
This method is more convenient and efficient than using `class` and `superclass` messages, as it allows you to query the class hierarchy directly.

Note that `isMemberOf:` is a more restrictive method that checks if an object is an instance of a specific class, without considering subclasses. For example:
```
1 isMemberOf: SmallInteger
>>> true
1 isMemberOf: Integer
>>> false
```
So, if you want to check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it, `isKindOf:` is the way to go.

title: How to print an integer to transcript
body:
I've tried the code

|myNum|
myNum := SmallInteger new: 0.
Transcript show: (myNum printString).

, but Pharo crashes upon running this code.
stackoverflow_answer:
You can't create a SmallInteger using new:. You already have the number you want to print. It's already a SmallInteger. Just use it like such:

Transcript show: 0 printString
explanation:
To print an integer to the transcript, you can use the following code:

`Transcript show: myNum printString.`

However, in your code, you are trying to print a `SmallInteger` object directly, which is not correct. The `printString` method is used to convert an object into a string representation, which can then be displayed in the transcript.

Here's the correct code:

```
| myNum |
myNum := SmallInteger new: 0.
Transcript show: myNum printString.
```

This code will create a new `SmallInteger` object with the value 0, convert it to a string using the `printString` method, and then display it in the transcript.

Note that the `Transcript` is an object that is often used for logging system messages, and it's a kind of system console (p. 16).

Also, if you want to print the result of an expression, you should use `Print it` instead of `Do it`. `Print it` compiles the expression, executes it, sends the message `printString` to the result, and displays the resulting string (p. 17-18).

For example:

```
3 + 4
>>> 7
```

Selecting `3+4` and printing it (CMD-p) will display the result `7` in the transcript.

Remember to use the notation `>>>` to indicate that a particular Pharo expression yields a given result when you print it (p. 18).

title: Regex: Matching all words in a phrase
body:
Is this possible?

For a sentence such as hello how are you, I'd like my regular expression to return hello how are you. It only ever returns just hello and not the other words.

My Regex: 

[A-Za-z]*

Any help is greatly appreciated. Thanks! If it matters, I'm using Pharo Smalltalk. I've been testing in c# too.
stackoverflow_answer:
If you only need split the sentence by spaces this can be done using string.Split() method:

var s = "hello how are you";
var words = s.Split();

If you want to use regular expressions:

var s = "hello how are you";
var regex = "\\w+";
var words = Regex.Matches(s, regex).Cast<Match>().Select(m => m.Value);
explanation:
The question asks if it's possible to match all words in a phrase using a regular expression. The answer is yes, it is possible. 

The regular expression `[A-Za-z]*` is not sufficient to match all words in a phrase because it only matches a single word. To match all words, you can use the regular expression `\w+` which matches one or more word characters (letters, digits, or underscores). 

In Pharo, you can use the `matchesCollect:` method to collect all matches of a regular expression within a string. For example, `'Jack meet Jill' regex: '\w+' matchesCollect: [:word | word]` will return an ordered collection of all words in the string (`OrderedCollection('Jack' 'meet' 'Jill')`). (p. 339)

Alternatively, you can use the `allRegexMatches:` method to get a collection of all matches of a regular expression within a string. For example, `'Jack and Jill went up the hill' allRegexMatches: '\w+'` will return an ordered collection of all words in the string (`OrderedCollection('Jack' 'and' 'Jill' 'went' 'up' 'the' 'hill')`). (p. 340)

Note that the `matchesCollect:` and `allRegexMatches:` methods return a collection of all matches, not just the first match. This is in contrast to the `matches:` method which only returns a boolean indicating whether the whole string matches the regular expression. (p. 341)

In summary, to match all words in a phrase, you can use the regular expression `\w+` and the `matchesCollect:` or `allRegexMatches:` methods in Pharo.

title: What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?
body:
In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

What is the difference between them?
stackoverflow_answer:
The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.

The implementation of yourself is

yourself
    ^self

meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.

The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

^receiver
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

receiver
   msg1;
   msg2.
^receiver

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.

^(self msg: arg1 arg: arg2)
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself.

Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:

| answer |
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
answer
   msg1;
   msg2.
^answer

which is a little bit more verbose.

To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.
explanation:
According to the textbook "Pharo By Example" (pp. 60, 103, 105), the difference between `self` and `yourself` in Smalltalk is that `yourself` is not a valid term in Smalltalk. The correct term is `self`, which represents the receiver of the message.

`self` is a pseudo-variable that represents the object inside which the current method is executing (p. 60). It is called the "receiver" because this object has received the message that caused the method to execute. `self` is not a keyword, but rather a variable that is bound to the respective receiver.

In a method, `self` represents the object that received the message, and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver (p. 103). This means that when a method is called, `self` will be bound to the object that received the message, and the method lookup will start in the class of that object.

On the other hand, `super` is also a pseudo-variable that represents the receiver of the message, but the method lookup starts in the superclass of the class of the method where the `super` send occurs (p. 105).

In summary, `self` and `super` both represent the receiver of the message, but they differ in the method lookup. `self` starts the lookup in the class of the receiver, while `super` starts the lookup in the superclass of the class of the method where the `super` send occurs.

Here is an example to illustrate the difference:
```
uniqueInstance
    ^ self
```
In this example, `self` will be bound to the respective receiver, whether it is `WebServer` or `ReliableWebServer`, and the method lookup will start in the class of the receiver (pp. 95-96).

In conclusion, `self` is a fundamental concept in Smalltalk that represents the receiver of the message, and it is essential to understand its role in method execution and lookup.

title: Smalltalk initialization variables
body:
In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.

How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?

I want something like 

|aColor|
aColor = Color new 'red'.

Or is this bad practice and should I always do

|aColor|
aColor = Color new.
aColor name:= red.d
stackoverflow_answer:
The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

aColor := Color named: 'Red'.

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

(class) Color>>named: aName
| color |
color := self new.
color name: aName.
^color.

Some things to note:

  • We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
  • We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
  • You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.

There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

[*] This is quite natural due to the orthogonal nature on Smalltalk, since everything (including classes) is an object. If you are interested check Chapter 13 of Pharo by Example or any other reference to classes and metaclasses in Smalltalk.

HTH
explanation:
In Pharo Smalltalk, you can initialize instance variables in several ways. One way is to use lazy initialization, which means delaying the initialization of a variable until it's actually needed (p. 6.6). This approach is useful when the variable is expensive to initialize or when users don't typically expect to call a specific method.

Another way is to use class variables, which are shared between instances and classes (p. 109). You can define class variables using the `classVariableNames:` keyword in the class definition (Listing 6-29). To initialize class variables, you can use an accessor method that initializes the variable if it hasn't been initialized before (p. 111).

In your example, you want to initialize an instance variable `aColor` with a specific value. You can do this using the `initialize` method, which is called automatically when an object is instantiated (p. 93).

Here's an example of how you can initialize `aColor`:
```
Color >> initialize
    aColor := Color new 'red'.
```
This approach is similar to constructors in languages like Java and C++. However, in Pharo Smalltalk, the `initialize` method is called automatically when an object is created, so you don't need to explicitly call it.

Alternatively, you can use lazy initialization to delay the initialization of `aColor` until it's actually needed. For example:
```
Color >> aColor
    ^ aColor ifNil: [aColor := Color new 'red']
```
This approach is useful when `aColor` is expensive to initialize or when you want to delay its initialization until it's actually needed.

It's worth noting that Pharo Smalltalk has a specific convention for naming variables, where instance variables start with a lowercase letter, and shared variables (including class variables and global variables) start with an uppercase letter (p. 109).
supporting_facts_labels: ['The initialize method is automatically executed after an object is created in Pharo .', 'The initialize method is defined on the instance side in the usual way ; remember that new automatically sends initialize to the newly - created instance .', 'This can be done by introducing an accessor method which , when executed , initializes the variable if it has not yet been initialized .', 'One solution is lazy initialization ( discussed earlier in this chapter ) .', 'Class variables : variables shared between instances and classes .']
supported_facts_labels: ["However , in Pharo Smalltalk , the ` initialize ` method is called automatically when an object is created , so you do n't need to explicitly call it .", 'You can do this using the ` initialize ` method , which is called automatically when an object is instantiated ( p. 93 ) .', "To initialize class variables , you can use an accessor method that initializes the variable if it has n't been initialized before ( p. 111 ) .", "One way is to use lazy initialization , which means delaying the initialization of a variable until it 's actually needed ( p. 6.6 ) .", 'Another way is to use class variables , which are shared between instances and classes ( p. 109 ) .']

title: For loop for array in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to make an array with random numbers (just 0 or 1), but when I run it, it just prints this: End of statement list encountered ->

This is my code:

GenList
 | lista |
  lista := Array new: 31.
  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

What can I do?
stackoverflow_answer:
Some interesting things to consider:

1. The method selector doesn't start with a lowercase letter

It is a tradition for selectors to start with a lowercase letter. In this sense, genLista would be more correct than GenLista.

2. The method selector includes the abbreviated word 'gen'

For instance, genLista could be renamed to genereLista o listaAlAzar (if you decide to use Spanish)

3. The Array named lista has 31 elements, not 30

The result of Array new: 31 is an array of 31 elements. However, the code below it only fills 30 of them, leaving the last one uninitialized (i.e., nil). Possible solution: lista := Array new: 30.

4. A dot is missing causing a compilation error

The code

  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

does not compile because there is no dot indicating the separation between the two sentences. Note that the error happens at compilation time (i.e., when you save the method) because the return token ^ must start a statement (i.e., it cannot be inlined inside a statement).

There are other cases where a missing dot will not prevent the code from compiling. Instead, an error will happen at runtime. Here is a (typical) example:

1 to: 10 do: [:i | self somethingWith: i]     "<- missing dot here"
self somethingElse

the missing dot will generate the runtime error self not understood by block.

5. There is a more expressive way of generating 0s and 1s at random

The calculation 2 atRandom - 1 is ok. However, it forces the reader to mentally do the math. A better way to reveal your intention would have been

#(0 1) atRandom

6. When playing with random numbers don't forget to save the seed

While it is ok to use atRandom, such a practice should only be used with "toy" code. If you are developing a system or a library, the recommended practice is to save the seed somewhere before generating any random data. This will allow you to reproduce the generation of random quantities later on for the sake of debugging or confirmation. (Note however, that this will not suffice for making your program deterministically reproducible because unordered (e.g. hashed) collections could form differently in successive executions.)
explanation:
According to the textbook "Pharo By Example", the issue with your code is that you're trying to access the array with indices starting from 1, but in Pharo, the first element of an array is at position 1, not 0 (p. 203).

To fix your code, you should change the loop to start from 1 and go up to 31, and use the `at:put:` method to set the values of the array. Here's the corrected code:
```
GenList
 | lista |
  lista := Array new: 31.
  1 to: 31 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista
```
This code will create an array of size 31, and then loop from 1 to 31, setting each element of the array to a random value of either 0 or 1 using the `at:put:` method.

Note that you can also use the `doWithIndex:` method, which gives access to the current index, like this:
```
GenList
 | lista |
  lista := Array new: 31.
  lista doWithIndex: [ :each :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista
```
This code uses the `doWithIndex:` method to iterate over the array, and sets each element to a random value of either 0 or 1 using the `at:put:` method.

Also, be careful not to modify literal arrays, as they are kept in compiled method literal frames and may not have the value you expect the second time you execute the code (p. 204).
supporting_facts_labels: ['Contrary to the C convention in Pharo , the first element of an array is at position 1 and not 0 .', 'Literal arrays are kept in compiled method literal frames ( a space where literals appearing in a method are stored ) , therefore unless you copy the array , the second time you execute the code your literal array may not have the value you expect .']
supported_facts_labels: ['According to the textbook " Pharo By Example " , the issue with your code is that you \'re trying to access the array with indices starting from 1 , but in Pharo , the first element of an array is at position 1 , not 0', 'as they are kept in compiled method literal frames and may not have the value you expect the second time you execute the code ( p. 204 )']

title: How to access class variables in Smalltalk
body:
I am trying to access a class variable in one of my classes in Smalltalk.

I have two classes: Class1 and Class2.

Class1 has the following variables: year month day hour minute. Class2 has the following variables: start-time end-time. In the initialize method for Class2 I have the following:

start-time := Class1 new.
end-time := Class1 new.

Now I want to assign the year 2012 to start-time, how do I access the year variable in the Class1 object start-time?
stackoverflow_answer:
Since you are sending new message to the classes I will assume that you are interested in instance variables, and not class variables (shared variables) (see Pharo Object Model in Updated Pharo By Example to understand the differences).

In Pharo all class/instance variables are private, thus the way to access them is to create accessors.

Add to your Class1 methods

Class1>>year
    ^ year

Class1>>year: aYear
    year := aYear

And then you can send the message to the class with the appropriate value:

Class2>>initialize
    startTime := Class1 new.
    startTime year: 2012.

    "or by using a cascade"
    startTime := Class1 new
        year: 2012;
        yourself.

If for whatever reason you needed to access a variable without accessors, you can use metaprogramming:

startTime instVarNamed: #year "meta-getter"
startTime instVarNamed: #year put: 2012 "meta-setter"

Finally, 'start-time' is not a valid variable name.
explanation:
To access a class variable in Smalltalk, you need to provide class methods to access them (p. 109). In your case, you want to assign the year 2012 to the `start-time` object, which is an instance of `Class1`. 

To access the `year` variable in the `Class1` object `start-time`, you can define a class method in `Class1` to access the `year` instance variable. For example, you can add a class method `year:` to `Class1` to set the `year` instance variable:

```smalltalk
Class1 class >> year: aYear
    ^ aYear
```

Then, you can use this class method to set the `year` instance variable of the `start-time` object:

```smalltalk
start-time := Class1 new.
start-time year: 2012.
```

Note that instance variables in Smalltalk are private to the instance itself (p. 89). This means that two instances of the same class cannot access each other's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods. There is no language syntax that provides direct access to the instance variables of any other object (p. 89).
supporting_facts_labels: ["In Pharo , two instances of the same class can not access each other 's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods .", 'There is no language syntax that provides direct access to the instance variables of any other object .']
supported_facts_labels: ['To access a class variable in Smalltalk , you need to provide class methods to access them ( p. 109 ) .', "This means that two instances of the same class can not access each other 's instance variables unless the class defines accessor methods .", 'There is no language syntax that provides direct access to the instance variables of any other object ( p. 89 ) .']

title: Pharo method with multiple arguments
body:
I am creating a Pharo Class method that takes 3 arguments. I am using the following code and it gives me the error "Variable or expression expected.."

    MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2
    ^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

What would be the correct method declaration syntax? Later on, I intend on calling this method like below :

    ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
stackoverflow_answer:
The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).

So for a method that, in Java or a similar "curly bracket language", might look something like this:

registerUser(String emailAddress, String password, boolean isAdmin) {...}

you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:

registerUserWithEmail: anEmailAddress password: aPassword isAdmin: aBoolean

making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a "symbol" in a global dictionary):

#registerUserWithEmail:password:isAdmin:

That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):

UserRegistry
    registerUserWithEmail: 'joe@bloggs.com'
    password: 'topSecret'
    isAdmin: true

Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.

Getting back to your case, you had this:

MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says "Variable or expression expected" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.

Some notes:

  • by convention, method names use lowerCamelCase
  • #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected; the same goes for #var1:var2:var3:
  • if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side
  • think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well; ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)
explanation:
According to the textbook "Pharo By Example", the correct method declaration syntax for a Pharo class method that takes 3 arguments is as follows (p. 64):

`MethodName: arg1: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3`

In your case, the correct declaration would be:

`MethodName: var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2`

This is because in Pharo, keyword messages take an arbitrary number of arguments (p. 63). When calling this method, you would use the same syntax:

`ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2`

Note that in Pharo, methods are normally defined in a browser window or in the debugger, and are developed one method at a time in the context of a given class (p. 64).

title: making a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk
body:
I want to implement a small class in Pharo so I have done this:

Object subclass: #Person
    instanceVariableNames: 'name age'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'Test'

and I want to simulate a constructor so I have made a method like the following:

newName:aName newAge:aAge
    "comment stating purpose of message"

    name:=aName.
    age:=aAge.
    ^self.

but when I want to call it from the playground in Pharo like this:

objPerson:=Person newName:'Carl' newAge: 10.

it is not recognized by Pharo, what am I doing wrong?
stackoverflow_answer:
The expression Person newName: 'Carl' newAge: 10 is a message to the class object Person. Therefore you have to implement it on the class side of Person.

Your code needs to be tweaked like this

Person class >> newName: aName newAge: anAge
  | person |
  person := self new.
  person name: aName.
  person age: anAge.
  ^person

Note that in the code above self refers to the class because the method is on the class side. But since person is an instance of Person, the messages name: aName and age: anAge have to be defined on the instance side.

Therefore, on the instance side, you need to add the two setters:

Person >> name: aString
  name := aString

Person >> age: anInteger
  age := anInteger

With these three methods you should be able to run your example.

Some additional comments on the coding style:

Firstly, I would have chosen a different selector for the "constructor" method (in Smalltalk we call these "instance creation" methods). For instance,

Person class >> named: aString age: anInteger
  ^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

Secondly, there is no need to use the temporary person for the newly created instance as the expression self new already refers to such an instance.

Finally, note the use of the cascade syntax in

^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

which means that the message age: anInteger will be sent to the same receiver that name: aString, which in this case happens to be the new instance returned by self new.
explanation:
To create a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk, you need to understand how classes and instances are initialized. According to the Pharo by Example textbook (p. 96), every class has a superclass, and it's good practice to send `super initialize` before performing any further initialization in the `initialize` method.

In your example, you've created a class `Person` with instance variables `name` and `age`, but you're not following the conventional way of initializing instances in Pharo.

To fix the issue, you should create an `initialize` method in your `Person` class, like this:
```smalltalk
Person >> initialize
    super initialize.
    name := ''.
    age := 0.
    ^ self
```
This method will be called automatically when you create a new instance of `Person` using `Person new`.

If you want to create a constructor-like method, you can define a class method `newName:newAge:` on `Person` class, like this:
```smalltalk
Person class >> newName: aName newAge: anAge
    ^ self new initialize; name: aName; age: anAge
```
This method creates a new instance of `Person`, initializes it, and sets the `name` and `age` instance variables.

Now, you can create a new `Person` instance in the playground like this:
```smalltalk
objPerson := Person newName: 'Carl' newAge: 10.
```
This should work as expected.

Remember, in Pharo, it's essential to follow the conventional way of initializing instances and classes to avoid unexpected behavior (p. 104).

title: SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript
body:
|oc|
oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
oc Transcript show: self; cr.

Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:

MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>> Transcript

When replacing 'self' with 'oc' I am still getting the same error. I am looking for a way to output the collection using the Transcript.

Why can't Transcript be the receiver of my code?
stackoverflow_answer:
Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
Transcript show: 'Show the collection:'; cr.
Transcript show: oc; cr.
Transcript show: 'Show each element:'; cr.
oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element; cr ].

Gives this output:

Show the collection:
an OrderedCollection(2 #(4 9))
Show each element:
2
#(4 9)
explanation:
According to the textbook "Pharo By Example" (pages 226-227, 204-205), the issue lies in the fact that `Transcript` is not a stream that can receive the message `show:` with an `OrderedCollection` as an argument.

To print the contents of an `OrderedCollection` object using `Transcript`, you need to use a `WriteStream` and send the `print:` message to it. Here's an example:
```smalltalk
| oc |
oc := OrderedCollection new.
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).

| stream |
stream := String new writeStream.
stream print: oc; cr.
Transcript show: stream contents.
```
This code creates an `OrderedCollection` object `oc`, adds elements to it, and then creates a `WriteStream` object `stream`. The `print:` message is sent to `stream` with `oc` as an argument, which adds the textual representation of `oc` to the stream. Finally, `Transcript` is sent the `show:` message with the contents of the stream as an argument, which prints the contents of `oc` to the Transcript.

Note that `Transcript` is a system console that is often used for logging system messages (page 16). It's not a stream that can receive the `show:` message with an `OrderedCollection` as an argument. Instead, you need to use a `WriteStream` to convert the `OrderedCollection` to a string, and then send that string to `Transcript` using the `show:` message.
supporting_facts_labels: ['226 11.3 Streaming over collections | stream | stream : = String new writeStream .']
supported_facts_labels: ['| stream |   stream : = String new writeStream .']

title: How do we write if elseif elseif in pharo?
body:
I have to check 3 conditons using the if elseif elseif. How can I do that in pharo. I did but am not sure since I did not find any such application in pharo.

x = 25
ifTrue:[DoSomething]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].

x < 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething]
ifFalse:[Domething else].

x > 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething ]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can choose different design (using polymorphism, lookup, ...) but that is pretty much the same for any OO language (for Smalltalk in particular, refer to this Refactoring if-chains in Smalltalk without class explosion ).

In Smalltalk (and couple other languages such as Ruby) you have one extra option and that is class extension. You can design your own "if" statements that match well your particular domain and makes the code more obvious.

E.g. in your given example, I could add a new method to the Number class called compareTo:lesser:equal:greater:, and then your code changes to

x compareTo: 25
    lesser: [ do something ]
    equals: [ do something else ]
    greater: [ do something entirely different ]

This naturally depends on your particular domain, maybe in different case the wording would be different. E.g. in case of collections, there's col ifEmpty: [ ] ifNotEmpty: [ ], for nil there's ifNil:ifNotNil:, for detection detect:ifFound:ifNone:, for dictionaries at:ifPresent:ifAbsent:, etc.
explanation:
According to the textbook "Pharo By Example" (pages 66, 314, 195-196), in Pharo, we write `if` `elseif` `elseif` using the following syntax:

`condition ifTrue: [block to execute if true] ifFalse: [block to execute if false]`

Here, `condition` is a boolean expression, and `[block to execute if true]` and `[block to execute if false]` are blocks of code to be executed depending on the condition.

To write an `if elseif elseif` construct, you can chain multiple `ifTrue:` and `ifFalse:` messages together. For example:

```
x < 25
    ifTrue: [DoSomething]
    ifFalse: [x > 25
        ifTrue: [DoSomethingElse]
        ifFalse: [DoAnotherThing]]
```

This code checks if `x` is less than 25, and if so, executes the block `[DoSomething]`. If not, it checks if `x` is greater than 25, and if so, executes the block `[DoSomethingElse]`. If not, it executes the block `[DoAnotherThing]`.

Note that Pharo does not have a special syntax for control constructs like `if` and `loop`. Instead, these are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections with blocks as arguments (pages 66, 314).
supporting_facts_labels: ['Instead , these are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans , numbers and collections , with blocks as arguments .']
supported_facts_labels: ['Instead , these are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans , numbers , and collections with blocks as arguments ( pages 66 , 314 ) .']

title: Usage of self subclassResponsibility
body:
I'm new to Pharo and I'm having trouble grasping some concepts, specifically subclassResponsibilty.

I have the following objecttree:

AbstractDictionary
--TreeBasedDictionary (not abstract)
--AbstractArrayDictionary
----SimpleDictionary (not abstract)
----FastDictionary (not abstract)

All 3 implement the operations at:put: and at: (and more). I understood from class today that I can pull up at:put: to the AbstractDictionary with the following implementation:

at: thisKey put: thisValue
"insert new key / value pair"
^self 
    at: thisKey
    put: thisValue
    duplicate: [self error:'duplicate key']

Then I implemented a metod at:put:duplicate: in TreeBasedDictionary and AbstractArrayDictionary (because the implementation is the same for the latter's subclasses). Then, when I call at:put: on an object it won't find it in the instance of either TreeBasedDictionary , SimpleDictionary or FastDictionary but when looking up the tree it finds this method in the superclass (AbstractDictionary). This will then call self with at:put:duplicate: . This method is implemented by the 3 afore mentioned classes, and then the self which is pointing to the instance of the class that made the initial call is sent the message to execute at:put:duplicate:.

Now, AbstractArrayDictionary,SimpleDictionary and FastDictionary all have a method named size. Both classes at the bottom of the inheritence tree implement this method. AbstractArrayDictionary implements this method as follows

size
"dictionary size"
self subclassResponsibility

Suppose I implemented this method for my TreeBasedDictionary, I suppose I should implement the size method in my AbstractDictionary class as shown above.

My questions: Why do I need to do it like that, and not like this:

size
"dictionary size"
^self size

And if so, do I remove the method from AbstractArrayDictionary and why?
stackoverflow_answer:
Ok, look.

First of all if you use 

size
  ^ self size

you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.

Now in general about what you should know.

  1. You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.
  2. You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.
  3. It's not nice to do things that I've written in #2 part. Because
    • when you browse AbstractDictionary class you get an idea that it should support at:put:duplicate:, but the functionality depends on the implementation (aka subclass).
    • when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error "message not understood by MagicBagDictionary".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement

size
  ^ container size

in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:

size
  "dictionary size"
  self subclassResponsibility

This way AbstractDictionary is saying:

yes, you can get my size, but I have no idea how to calculate it because I'm not complete. But my subclasses should know that, so don't hesitate to ask.
explanation:
Here's a detailed, concise, pertinent, and coherent explanatory answer to your question:

**Why `subclassResponsibility` is used**

In Pharo, `subclassResponsibility` is a convention used to signal that a subclass should have implemented a particular method (p. 97, 113). It's a way to announce that the subclass should have overridden a specific method. When a method sends `self subclassResponsibility`, it means that the method is abstract and should be implemented by its subclasses (p. 311).

**How it works**

When a method sends `self subclassResponsibility`, it raises an error indicating that the subclass should have overridden the method (p. 187). This error message is provided by asking `thisContext` for the sender, which allows Pharo to provide a useful error message indicating which abstract method has been invoked (p. 311).

**Why not implement `size` as `^self size`**

Implementing `size` as `^self size` would not work as expected because it would simply recurse indefinitely. By convention, methods that send `self subclassResponsibility` are considered abstract, and should never be executed (p. 97). If you implement `size` in `AbstractDictionary` as `^self size`, it would call itself recursively, leading to an infinite loop.

**Why implement `size` in `AbstractArrayDictionary` and not in `AbstractDictionary`**

Implementing `size` in `AbstractArrayDictionary` and not in `AbstractDictionary` is a design choice. Since `AbstractArrayDictionary` is a more specific subclass of `AbstractDictionary`, it's reasonable to implement `size` in `AbstractArrayDictionary` if its subclasses (e.g., `SimpleDictionary` and `FastDictionary`) need to provide their own implementation of `size`. By implementing `size` in `AbstractArrayDictionary`, you ensure that its subclasses will provide their own implementation of `size`.

**Removing the method from `AbstractArrayDictionary`**

If you implement `size` in `AbstractDictionary` as `self subclassResponsibility`, then you should remove the implementation of `size` from `AbstractArrayDictionary`. This is because `AbstractArrayDictionary` is a subclass of `AbstractDictionary`, and it should not provide its own implementation of `size` if the abstract method is already implemented in `AbstractDictionary`.

In summary, `subclassResponsibility` is a convention used to signal that a subclass should have implemented a particular method. It's used to provide a useful error message indicating which abstract method has been invoked. Implementing `size` in `AbstractDictionary` as `self subclassResponsibility` is a way to announce that subclasses should implement their own `size` method.
supporting_facts_labels: ['* Return self subclassResponsibility to make a method abstract .', 'Instead , by convention , the body of an abstract method consists of the expression self subclassResponsibility .']
supported_facts_labels: ['When a method sends ` self subclassResponsibility ` , it means that the method is abstract and should be implemented by its subclasses ( p. 311 ) .', 'By convention , methods that send ` self subclassResponsibility ` are considered abstract , and should never be executed ( p. 97 ) .']

title: Syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0
body:
I am learning Pharo online and am not sure if I got the syntax correct for creating class and instance variables. Please correct me if I am wrong :-

Class (Static) method created on class side of Pharo, where name, email, phone are instance variables of class CreateUser:

createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone

To call this static method of class CreateUser, I will do the following :-

CreateUser 
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

If I want to create an instance variable by this name, the method declaration will be exactly the same as above, but it will be on the instance side of the class. However, when I call the method, I will call it using the keyword "new" to create a new instance as under:

CreateUser new
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

When I run the above code and call this method statically, I get an error message as:-

MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >>name:email:phone:

However, when I go to the CreateUser class to recheck, I see the above method create on the class side as : 

CreateUser:name:email:phone:

My queries are as below: 1. What am I doing wrong above? How can I fix the above error? 2. The concept behind using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods is the same as Java? 3. If I wish to access the above instance variables, I can add accessor methods for class/instance and then call them with the class instance/class object instance. Is that correct?

Any help you can give will be greatly appreciated! Thanks very much in advance.
stackoverflow_answer:
I guess you misunderstand the method syntax a bit, because createNewUser:Arguments part makes no sense. What you should have is a method on the class side like this:

name: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   "and here you probably have something like:"
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   "and so on"

In my example name:email:phone: is the method's selector and userName, userEmail and userPhone are parameters. You can call this method as in your example. name and email are either class side on instance side variables depending on where the method is defined.

Also you shouldn't name a class CreateUser. Think about this, what will be the instances called? "createUsers"? Usually you name a class User, then you can think about instances as "users", and then the responsibility of the class object is "to create users (its instances)".

Please note, that it's weird to have a method like this on the class side. What you usually do is to create an instance method:

initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   phone := userPhone

and a class side method:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   | instance |
   instance := self new.
   instance initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone.
   ^ instance

or a shorter version using cascaded messages:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   ^ self new
      initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone;
      yourself

2) In Pharo (and Smalltalk) the concept is a bit simpler. As everything is an object, Class is an object also, so class side variables and methods are instance variables and methods of a class which is an instance of the "class class". Next picture may help you to understand the associations between objects in Pharo:

This may be a bit confusing at the beginning, but in the end, you don't have and static/nonstatic methods/variables, you just have objects, instantiation and inheritance.

So what you should think about is what are you going to ask the objects about. Probably you should ask User about it's email or mobile number, but you will ask User class to create a user or find a user, or suggest a default t-shirt size for a user.

3) Yes, you should make an accessor. Moreover, if you select a class in system browser and press Cmd+H+A (or Ctrl, or Alt) depending on your OS, you will get a dialog for automatic accessor creation
explanation:
I'll address each of your queries and provide explanations with references to the Pharo By Example textbook.

**1. What am I doing wrong above? How can I fix the above error?**

The error `MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >>name:email:phone:` indicates that Pharo doesn't understand the message `name:email:phone:` sent to the `CreateUser` class. This is because the syntax for defining a class method in Pharo is different from what you've written.

To fix this error, you should define a class method with the correct syntax. For example, if you want to create a class method `createNewUser:` that takes three arguments `name:`, `email:`, and `phone:`, you would define it as follows:
```smalltalk
CreateUser class >> createNewUser: name: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
    "implementation of the method"
```
**2. The concept behind using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods is the same as Java?**

No, the concept is not the same as Java. In Pharo, class methods are dynamically-dispatched methods, which means that inheritance, overriding, and super-sends work for class methods, unlike static methods in Java (p. 92).

**3. If I wish to access the above instance variables, I can add accessor methods for class/instance and then call them with the class instance/class object instance. Is that correct?**

Yes, that's correct. In Pharo, instance variables can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them, and also in the methods defined in its subclasses (p. 89). However, if you want to access instance variables from outside the class, you need to define accessor methods. For example, if you have an instance variable `name` in the `CreateUser` class, you can define an accessor method `name` that returns the value of the instance variable:
```smalltalk
CreateUser >> name
    ^ name
```
Then, you can call this method on an instance of the `CreateUser` class to access the `name` instance variable.

Additional notes:

* In Pharo, comments are enclosed in double quotes `"` (p. 61).
* Local variable definitions use vertical bars `| |` (p. 61).
* Assignment uses `:=` (p. 61).
* Blocks are defined using square brackets `[]` (p. 61).
* Class instance variables are private to each class and are inherited by subclasses, but each subclass has its own private copies of those variables (p. 92).

I hope this helps!

title: If my Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, how do I break out of the loop?
body:
Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!

I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?
stackoverflow_answer:
Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.

If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the

  • „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
  • open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.
explanation:
If your Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, you can break out of the loop by typing `CMD-.` (that's a full stop or a period, depending on where you learned English) to interrupt the computation and open a debugger on the execution (p. 140). Alternatively, you can also edit the suspect code to insert `Halt now` and run the method, which will bring up the pre-debugger, from where you can either proceed or go into the debugger (p. 140).

Once you are in the debugger, you can examine the execution and find the problematic code. You can then fix the code and continue or quit the debugger.

Additionally, if you want to conditionally halt only in a specific context, you can use `Object>>haltIf:` method, which allows you to set a breakpoint that only triggers in a specific context (pp. 311-312).

It's also important to note that when you quit Pharo, you will normally save a snapshot that contains all of your objects, so you can recover your work even if you lost it due to an infinite loop (p. 15).

In your case, since you lost all your work for the past two hours, you can try to recover it by restarting the image and saving it again. Pharo will display a prompt to ask you about saving your image, and if you do save your image and reopen it, you will see that things are exactly as you left them (p. 15).
